text: were seen as standing in contrast to the teaching of Christ, and of course the games were still connected to the veneration of the traditional gods. Christians were advised not to participate in the festivities. But the rejection of these gaming activities also went deeper: it had to do with the negative moral sentiments that they served to stir. The Christian condemnation of play and gambling has to many ‘moderns’ seemed outdated and ridiculous; but one has to understand very carefully what it is that is being rejected, and on what grounds. The early Christians were not against people having a ‘good time’; they literally saw the ‘devil at play’ in the mass-intoxication and fixation with violence, but for obvious reasons, such a critique would easily end in some form of puritan position. This schismogenetic development would remain a constant theme in European history ever since, although it is clear that today the puritan positions have retreated to a minority position without much in the way of political influence. The more general point can be stated in simple terms. If play, pace Huizinga, relates to the creation of culture and is, as Elias argued, intimately linked with the civilizing process, the inverse argument can also be made: a derailed attitude to play is just as intimately part of any de-civilizing process. As Mumford noted with characteristic precision: ‘These are symptoms of the end: magnifications of demoralized power, minifications of life. When these signs multiply, Necropolis is near, though not a stone has yet crumbled. For the barbarian has already captured the city from within. Come hangman! Come vulture!’ (Mumford 1961: 242). It would take a thousand years to recover. Game and Gambling in Early Modernity: The Role of Venice Let us travel with seven-league boots to the fifteenth century, staying however within the Italian peninsula, more precisely Venice and surroundings, the birth- place of institutionalized gambling. The analytical perspectives proposed here in many ways run parallel to the recent argument made by Arpad Szakolczai in Comedy and the Public Sphere (2013), where he suggests a radically different genealogy of the public sphere, moving completely outside established traditions in sociology and political theory. The general point is that we have overlooked a series of cultural practices that became foundational for the emergence of the modern public sphere (or rather, ‘stage’), centuries before the Parisian coffee Liminality and the Modern 156 salons. Szakolczai focuses for very good reasons on the role played by comedy in the late Renaissance or early modernity, as mimes, clowns and comedians came to conquer public squares and spaces. From here, he shows, new forms of ‘denuding’ critique and sarcasm became possible, but often with devastating effects on ordinary and meaningful sociability. The ‘public sphere’, as we are starting to realize these days, is not always such a terribly nice thing, and it can, like markets or states, become perverted by all sorts of power and identity politics. This recognition was in fact also made by Kierkegaard in his astonishingly clear- cut understanding, in the middle of the nineteenth century, that the modern public sphere as produced by the media, was establishing itself as a stage for reciprocal humiliation and de-humanization. The role that gaming and gambling had on this cultural construction of the public stage should not be overlooked. Gaming practices revolutionized cultural and social life in the early modern period, and with particularly rapid effects across the European continent. These cultural developments had enormous impacts on the economy; and they ran parallel to developments in political history that here we can here merely hint at. Venice: Merchant and Ludic Capital of Early Modernity Venice is itself a liminal place in more than one way. It was built on the edge of the inhabitable world, and the building started unplanned. The Roman empire had been devastated by barbarians, and there was no overarching control of the adjacent territories. It was built in the shelter of the lagoon, as a quintessentially interstitial urbanity. It was also due to its in-between position between Europe and the East that it could establish itself as Europe’s leading trade empire. Venice is today a famous tourist attraction. However, the endless beauty of the city should not conceal its historical importance. As discussed in detail by Szakolczai, it was the place where the mimes of Constantinople arrived. It was also in Venice that in 1441 the first recorded large-scale masked festivity took place, establishing the carnival tradition with which the city is still identified today. It was furthermore in Venice that the first staged comedy was performed, in 1431 (Szakolczai 2013: 165). As Szakolczai puts it, ‘Venice was destined to pioneer the rebirth of theatre’ (ibid.: 166), characterized as it was by a manifest sense of ‘theatricality’, omnipresent in the architecture, urban texture and cultural life of the city. Venice’s role as nurturer of the modern public sphere was further underlined by the fact that this was one of the first places in Europe where the printing press started to function. Venice quickly became a centre for the early modern regime of information and communication, following the invention of printing with movable type (Burke 2000). It was precisely because of its liminal position that it achieved this centrality. Venice occupied the position similar to that which California has come to gain in more recent times as a global centre for the joint development of information technology (Silicon Valley), the entertainment industry, and Game and Gambling and the Implosion of Liminality: Playing Modernity 157 a spreading culture of personal ‘freedoms’. It should also not be forgotten that modern banking practices emerged in Venice, and it was from here that the first in a long series of financial crackdowns started. From the period of the First Crusade in 1099 through to the collapse and depopulation of the Black Death in 1347–1351, Europe was in many ways dominated by the Venetian empire.
